query: effect of rotavirus vaccines on rotavirus genotype distribution

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ten different species of rotavirus are currently classified (A-J). Species A rotaviruses are the most common cause of childhood infections, with species B and C also causing a smaller but significant percentage of infections across the world. [2] [3] Geographical variances in the strains of species A rotaviruses have been seen circulating globally. [1] Rotavirus disease epidemiology has drastically changed since the development of vaccines against the virus. Prior to vaccine development, rotavirus infection was most common in children younger than 5. Following widespread vaccination programs, rotavirus appears to be more prevalent in older, unvaccinated children. In low-income countries where rotavirus vaccines are not widespread, the prevalence of rotavirus infections appears to be stable. Malnutrition in these regions tends to increase the severity of disease. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Rotavirus infections are significantly more lethal in low-income countries when compared with high-income countries. Additionally, the efficacy of vaccines is decreased in low-income countries when compared to high-income countries. Factors such as climate, poverty, poor nutrition, inadequate sanitation, and prevalence of diseases are known to decrease the efficacy of vaccines. Further studies are necessary to determine what alterations can be made to vaccine delivery systems and to better address the vaccination need in low-income areas worldwide.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Several vaccines have been developed for the prevention of rotavirus over the years. Most of the vaccines are live-attenuated variants of naturally occurring strains. An early vaccine for rotavirus was removed from the market due to an increased risk of intussusception. A newer vaccine is a monovalent human vaccine given in two doses from 6 to 24 weeks. A pentavalent bovine-human reassortant vaccine is also available. It is given in 3 doses from 6 to 32 weeks. Analysis of both newer vaccines shows decreased vaccine efficacy in patients from high-mortality regions when compared to low-mortality regions. Both vaccines are considered safe and have no significant increase in adverse events between vaccine and placebo groups. China, India, and Vietnam have vaccines that are licensed locally, and additional vaccines are being evaluated in clinical trials across the world.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Gastroenteritis -- Etiology -- Rotavirus
retrieved_docs.content: Before vaccination, the United States saw an estimated 55,000 to 70,000 hospitalizations of children younger than 5 due to rotavirus. According to the CDC, this number has decreased by 40,000 to 50,000 since the vaccine became available. Although the incidence of rotavirus in the United States has been historically seasonal, with peak seasonality being from December to April, this pattern has become very inconsistent since vaccination became common. [13] Now, the virus tends to infect individuals in the United States sporadically throughout the year. Despite the widespread use of the vaccine in developed countries, rotavirus is still the leading cause of infantile diarrheal illness worldwide, with the CDC estimating that 215,000 rotavirus-related deaths occurred in 2013. Although there has been a continued decline in disease burden, some regions of the world are still disproportionately affected by this virus. [14] More than 40% of World Health Organization member countries have initiated large-scale vaccination of children. This number is anticipated to rise in the next few years. In turn, infection and mortality caused by rotavirus are expected to continue declining. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Rotavirus ", for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: On a global scale, there are significant disparities in healthcare outcomes between low-income and high-income countries due to the lack of clean water, the absence of resources to maintain adequate hygiene and sanitization, poor education, and the absence of healthcare facilities and vaccine programs. Many low-income countries lack the necessary resources to prevent disease outbreaks, and rotavirus vaccines are not as readily available in these regions. Most rotavirus vaccines are stable only in climate-controlled environments, making it difficult to distribute to temperate regions of the globe where the mortality rate is the highest.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Gastroenteritis -- Etiology -- Rotavirus
retrieved_docs.content: Throughout recorded history, rotavirus has been the leading cause of episodic infantile illness worldwide. However, in 2006, an oral vaccine was introduced. Since the introduction and utilization of this vaccine, the United States and many other industrialized countries have seen a sharp decline in the number and severity of gastroenteritis cases caused by rotavirus. Before 2006, studies estimated that over 3.5 million infants were affected annually in the United States and that rotavirus led to 440,000 deaths annually worldwide in children younger than 5. [9] Since the routine vaccination of children, each year has seen a significant reduction in cases. [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Gastroenteritis (Nursing) -- Causes -- Rotavirus
retrieved_docs.content: Throughout recorded history, rotavirus has been the leading cause of episodic infantile illness worldwide. However, in 2006 an oral vaccine was introduced. Since the introduction and utilization of this vaccine, the United States and many other industrialized countries have seen a sharp decline in the number and severity of gastroenteritis cases caused by rotavirus. Before 2006, it was estimated that over 3.5 million infants were affected annually in the United States and that rotavirus led to 440,000 deaths annually worldwide in children less than five years old [9] . Since the routine vaccination of children, each year has seen a 58% to 90% reduction in cases [10] . Before vaccination, the United States saw an estimated 55,000-70,000 hospitalizations of children under five due to rotavirus. This number has decreased by 40,000-50,000 since the vaccine became available, according to the Centers for Disease Control. Although the incidence of rotavirus in the United States has been historically seasonal, with peak seasonality being from December to April, this pattern has become very inconsistent since vaccination became common [11] . Now, the virus tends to infect sporadically throughout the year in the United States. Despite the widespread use of the vaccine in developed countries, rotavirus is still the leading cause of infantile diarrheal illness worldwide. The Centers for Disease Control estimated that there were still 215,000 rotavirus-related deaths in 2013. More than 40% of World Health Organization member countries have initiated large-scale vaccination of children. This number is anticipated to rise in the next few years. In turn, infection and mortality caused by rotavirus are expected to continue declining.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Gastroenteritis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Before routine vaccination, rotavirus was the most common cause of diarrheal illness in the pediatric population, with roughly 3.5 million cases yearly in the United States. Nearly all children possessed rotavirus antibodies by age 3. Worldwide, rotavirus accounted for 440,000 deaths per year. [9] However, since the implementation of vaccination in 2006, the number of cases seen annually in the United States has declined 50% to 90% per year. [13] As more countries adopt the standard practice of rotavirus vaccination, the overall number of cases is expected to continue to decrease. Other viral causes, eg, adenovirus, sapovirus, and astrovirus, account for 2% to 9% of cases worldwide, with a higher bias for children than adults. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Rotavirus is a double-stranded ribonucleic acid virus, named for its classic “wheel-shaped” appearance on electron microscopy (see Image. Rotavirus). [1] Transmission of rotavirus primarily occurs through the fecal-oral route. Additionally, the viral spread can occur through contaminated hands, fomites, and, rarely, food and water. [3] Prior to the routine use of the rotavirus vaccines, the rates of rotavirus illness among children in low-income countries were similar to those of high-income countries. [2] Since the advent of rotavirus vaccines, norovirus has exceeded rotavirus as the leading cause of acute viral gastroenteritis in high-income countries. In low-income countries and globally, rotavirus infections are the number one cause of acute viral gastroenteritis in children.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Rotavirus is the leading cause of severe gastroenteritis in children younger than 5 years of age. In 1973, rotavirus was discovered from duodenal biopsies and fecal samples taken from humans with acute diarrhea. Despite the availability of a vaccine against rotavirus, it continues to result in more than 200,000 deaths worldwide per year. In developed countries with routine vaccination programs, rotavirus infection is less prevalent than in non-developed countries, where it continues to be a major cause of life-threatening diarrhea in infants and children younger than 5. Rotavirus symptoms include profuse diarrhea, vomiting, fever, malaise, and rarely neurologic features such as convulsions, encephalitis, or encephalopathy. The most common symptoms are diarrhea and vomiting, leading to significant dehydration and reduced oral intake, which can necessitate hospitalization and lead to death if not treated. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: To combat rotavirus mortality on a global scale, it is necessary to invest in research and vaccine development that targets the specific needs of every population impacted by this infection. This includes the low-income countries where healthcare resources and funding are lacking. Every attempt should be made to ensure that these regions of the globe have access to adequate nutrition, clean water, necessary hygiene products, and appropriate healthcare facilities to combat the illness. Only when these advancements are made will we see a truly significant decrease in global mortality from rotavirus infections. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Rotavirus Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: Six weeks is the minimum age for the first dose of rotavirus. Three dose series recommended at 2,,4 and 6 months for pentavalent rotavirus vaccine. A 2-dose series at 2 and 4 months is recommended if the monovalent rotavirus vaccine is administered. The series should not be started after 15 weeks of age. The final dose should be administered before 8 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Lab testing is generally not performed, but it is the only way to confirm the diagnosis of rotavirus. In severe, intractable cases of infection, a confirmatory lab test may be indicated to solidify the diagnosis of rotavirus. [3] When a laboratory-confirmed diagnosis is desired, a rotavirus antigen can be found in stool samples using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay or immunochromatography. The addition of reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction assays is more sensitive and allows genotyping of virus isolates and as such, may be indicated in epidemiological studies. [1] Additional methods of detection include electron microscopy, polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis, antigen detection assays, and virus isolation. [3] In most cases, confirmation testing is only indicated if it could potentially cut costs by decreasing hospital stays or avoiding unnecessary procedures. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: All of the currently available vaccines for rotavirus infection are live attenuated vaccines. These are not without risk, as evidenced by the increased risk of intussusception with the first vaccine's administration. Since the removal of the earliest vaccine from the market, various studies have been performed to evaluate the factors that lead to an increased risk of intussusception. The incidence of intussusception is increased 3 to 14 days after vaccine administration, particularly following the first dose of the vaccine. Additional studies showed that the administration of the vaccine in infants greater than 90 days old led to over 80% of cases of intussusception. Studies that evaluate the risk of the currently available vaccines in various age groups are ongoing. Overall, the benefit of rotavirus vaccination clearly outweighs the risks of adverse events, including intussusception. To further decrease the risk of intussusception, vaccination should be given to individuals in two doses and less than 60 days of age. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotavirus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Rotaviruses are found throughout the world, and most children are infected by 5 years of age. The frequency of infection is similar throughout the world; however, fatal infection is more likely in low-income regions throughout the globe. This is likely secondary to the lack of adequate healthcare facilities, increased rates of malnutrition, and lack of access to clean, sanitary hydration therapies. [1] Rotavirus is traditionally thought of as a winter disease, especially in more temperate areas of the globe. [4] In tropical climates, the seasonal trend is less evident, and infections tend to occur year-round. Some research has shown that the frequency of rotavirus infection in temperate climates may also be related to precipitation levels. One study out of Washington, DC revealed that there was a 45% increase in rotavirus hospitalizations following months with low precipitation compared to months with higher levels of precipitation. This indicates that rotavirus transmission is more common in conditions of low humidity, as seen in winter and dry months throughout the year. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2351
retrieved_docs.content: Vibrio cholerae, the gram‑negative bacterium that causes cholera, is directly associated with cholera vaccines, which are formulated to induce immunity against this pathogen and thereby prevent the severe diarrheal disease it produces. Understanding the link between Vibrio cholerae and cholera vaccines is essential for developing effective immunization strategies, guiding public‑health interventions, and reducing the incidence of cholera outbreaks worldwide.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: Toxicological considerations also extend to the development, evaluation, and administration of viral vaccines, requiring vigilance for any toxic risks associated with vaccine components or adjuvants. Comprehensive awareness of the toxic profiles of these agents, together with systematic application of the Allowed qualifier, is essential for optimizing patient care and ensuring the safety of therapeutic and preventive interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the combined bacterial and viral vaccines group, meaning it incorporates antigens that protect against both bacterial infections (such as diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis) and viral infections (such as poliomyelitis). By belonging to this category of combination vaccines, the DTPP vaccine enables simultaneous immunization against multiple pathogens, streamlining vaccination schedules and improving patient adherence to preventive care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial vaccines represent a broad class of immunizations that target bacterial pathogens, and cholera vaccines are a specific type within this class, illustrating the hierarchical relationship in which the general category of bacterial vaccines encompasses the more specialized cholera vaccine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1495
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vakcíny” is classified under the broader category of viral vaccines, indicating that viral vaccines serve as the parent classification for these vaccines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 95
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vakcíny” (vaccines) is positioned within a broader taxonomy that includes both “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined” and the more general category “medications and vaccines,” indicating that vaccines are considered a subset of combined bacterial‑viral immunizations as well as of pharmaceutical products overall. Within the vaccine hierarchy, “toxoidy” is identified as a child category of “vakcíny,” representing toxoid‑based vaccines as a specific type of vaccine. Individual immunizations are linked to these categories: the “SNC‑rCTB vaccine” is mapped to the overarching “vakcíny” class, and the “DTPP vaccine” is recognized as a member of the “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined” group. This structured relationship clarifies how vaccines are organized into broader medication groups, how sub‑types such as toxoid vaccines fit within the overall vaccine category, and how specific vaccines are classified within combined bacterial‑viral immunization sets, supporting coherent medical classification and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 409
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccines (vakcíny) represent a broad class of immunizations that include several specialized subcategories. Combined bacterial and viral vaccines, referred to as “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined,” are a distinct group within the overall vaccine category, and the DTPP vaccine is an example that belongs to this combined bacterial‑viral group. Toxoidy (toxoid vaccines) is a child subcategory of vaccines, focusing on inactivated toxins as immunogens. Viral vaccines constitute another subclass of vaccines; the active ingredients of these formulations are viral antigens (antigeny virové). The SNC‑rCTB vaccine is mapped to the general vaccine class, indicating that it is also a vaccine. Together, these relationships illustrate how various vaccine types—combined bacterial/viral formulations, toxoid preparations, and viral antigen‑based products—are organized under the overarching concept of vaccines, highlighting their shared purpose of preventing infectious diseases and clarifying how specific products such as DTPP and SNC‑rCTB fit within this taxonomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is identified as a therapeutic intervention that includes immunization, injections, and perfusion methods. This specific preventive measure is placed under the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, indicating that it has a parent relationship to that general class. The overarching class of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions to classify and organize these preventive immunization strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1409
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial and viral vaccines, combined are recognized as a specific subclass within the broader category of vakcíny (vaccines), indicating that these combined formulations belong to the overall vaccine family. Within vakcíny, toxoidy represents a child category, denoting that toxoid vaccines are a distinct type of vaccine derived from inactivated toxins. The SNC‑rCTB vaccine is mapped to vakcíny, confirming its classification as a vaccine. Additionally, the DTPP vaccine is identified as a member of the bacterial and viral vaccines, combined group, illustrating that it is an example of a combined bacterial‑viral vaccine. Together, these relationships highlight the hierarchical organization of vaccines, encompassing combined bacterial‑viral formulations, toxoid vaccines, and individual products such as SNC‑rCTB and DTPP within the comprehensive vaccine taxonomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1388
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a kombinované vakcíny (combined vaccine), indicating that it incorporates multiple antigens into a single immunization preparation. This categorization highlights the vaccine’s role in delivering protection against several diseases—such as diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, and polio—through one combined formulation, which can simplify vaccination schedules and improve patient compliance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: The NICHD Terminology encompasses a range of medical concepts, including the influenza vaccine (vakcíny proti chřipce) and bacterial gastroenteritis (التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي). Within its subsets, the terminology also contains the concept الهَلْوَسة and the condition حالة ارتباكية (confusional state). By incorporating both preventive interventions such as the influenza vaccine and disease entities like bacterial gastroenteritis, as well as specific clinical descriptors such as الهَلْوَسة and حالة ارتباكية, the NICHD Terminology provides a comprehensive framework for categorizing and referencing diverse health‑related topics.

retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings are classified within the broader domain of immunizations and screening for infectious disease, indicating that these atypical laboratory results are considered part of preventive health assessments. Within this category, the specific test known as تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis) is identified as a child concept, representing a particular type of cytogenetic evaluation that falls under the umbrella of other and nonspecific abnormal test findings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: The NICHD Terminology includes a specific subset that encompasses several distinct medical concepts. Within this subset, the concept of tube feeding (التغذية بالأنبوب) is represented, reflecting the importance of enteral nutrition support in clinical care. The same subset also includes the concept of influenza vaccines (vakcíny proti chřipce), underscoring the role of immunization in preventing seasonal viral illness. Additionally, the subset contains the concept of bacterial intestinal infection (التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي), highlighting the relevance of gastrointestinal infectious diseases. Together, these concepts illustrate the breadth of the NICHD Terminology, covering nutritional interventions, preventive immunization, and infectious disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1220
retrieved_docs.content: Vibrio cholerae, the bacterium that causes cholera, is directly associated with cholera vaccines, indicating that these vaccines are designed to target this specific pathogen. Cholera vaccines are classified as a subset of bacterial vaccines, placing them within the broader category of immunizations that protect against bacterial infections. Understanding that Vibrio cholerae is the target of cholera vaccines and that these vaccines belong to the larger group of bacterial vaccines helps guide vaccine development, selection, and public health strategies aimed at preventing cholera and other bacterial diseases.
